title: My Quarto Website
text:
Richa Gandhi





Graduated from the University of Texas at Dallas with a Bachelor’s in International Political Economy, and am finishing an accelerated Master’s degree in Social Data Analytics and Research in May 2026. My work in data and strategy ranges from analyzing the intersection of AI, Cybersecurity, and federal policies to driving customer engagement strategies in the private sector and advancing operational efficiency in city government. I excel in translating complex research and analytics into actionable insights that support evidence based decision making. Through these experiences, I’ve built expertise at the intersection of data, policy, and strategy, helping organizations streamline processes, solve problems, and deliver impact. My professional and academic background fuels my passion for strategic decision making and data driven problem solving.
Interests:
Data Science/Analytics | AI & Cybersecurity Policy | Qualitative & Quantitative Research Methodologies | Stakeholder Advisory | Data Storytelling & Visualization | Public/Private Partnerships

title: About
text:
About this site
This site is used to display all the work I’ve done for my Information Management course and past projects throughout my master’s degree.


M.S SDAR Projects
Methods of Data Collection and Production (Fall 2024)

Social Media & Influencer Impact on Fast Fashion

Description: This research project explores the intersection of consumer behavior and social media marketing within the fast fashion industry. Using a mixed methods approach, my team integrated web scraping via YouTube APIs and TikTok with a structured Qualitative survey to analyze how purchased content is influenced by influencer content. By processing social media metadata and developing an R-based data pipeline to harvest, we identified a significant 2024 surge in influencer lead “spree” and “haul” content. The study highlights Gen Z consumers increasingly bypass “identification of need” and shifting in the traditional decision-making model. Consumers phase in favoring impulse purchases driven by perceived influencer trustworthiness, authenticity, and urgency. The project’s technical scope involves two prolonged analyses of latent marketing effects and conscious consumer opinions. Through word frequency distributions (Word Clouds) and sentiment analysis of scraped TiktTok adinsights and YouTube descriptions, we mapped thematic clusters that define modern fast fashion in the trade-off between “cheap” pricing and “quality pricing.” Despite the challenges of navigating data sparsity and API rate limits, the findings underscore that the parasocial connection and high energy of influencers remain effective for brand expansion and are effective drivers in the digital age.
Research Design (Fall 2025)

Project A: Gamifying Transit: Raffle-Based Incentives for the MBTA

Description: This research proposal explores behavioral economics at the intersection of increasing public transportation ridership, as it is designed to do so within the Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority (MBTA) system. With higher rewards for off-peak travel to balance network load, the study suggests implementing a mobile-app-based raffle system where commuters earn tickets for purchasing fares, which is grounded in Prospect Theory. By shifting the incentive structure from guaranteed small discounts to high reward lottery (such as local business gift cards and monthly LinkPasses), the project aims to take risks for perceived high-value gains and leverage human tendency, a strategy is proven successful in the INSINC program in Singapore for transit models. A crossover experimental design for the proposed methodology was utilized for the 2026-2027 schedule. The experimental and control groups are assigned to specific subway lines (Orange/Green vs. Blue/Red). By collecting revenue data and monthly ridership and comparing it against 2025 pre-test baselines, the study seeks to determine if gamification can provide a cost-effective solution to the MBTA’s budget deficits while reducing social and environmental costs of private vehicle reliance. This project demonstrates a high-level application of causal inference, experimental design, and urban policy planning.

Project B: Impact of Student Organizations on Campus Belonging

Description: This study investigates the correlation between psychological sense of belonging and student organization participation at the University of Texas at Dallas (Utd). By utilizing an adapted version of the Psychological Sense of School Membership (PSSM) survey, our team collected data from 49 graduate and undergraduate students to measure variables across four categories: teacher-student relationships, sense of acceptance, sense of attachment, and peer relationships. Using Google Sheets for heat maps and data processing for visual analysis, we were able to calculate Likert scores to compare non-participants with participants. Our findings led us to fail to reject the null hypothesis, as the average belonging score for non-participants was 41.15 and nearly identical for participants with a score of 41.31. However, when looking at the data and doing analysis, it revealed that undergraduate students who were in exactly two organizations reported a higher sense of belonging as opposed to those who were in fewer or more groups. The most consistent positive outcomes for campus attachment and teacher-student relations were observed when participation in these organizations frequency was done specifically twice a month or daily. Overall, this project highlights “quality over quantity,” and student integration and campus involvement can play a pivotal role in student retention.
The Leadership Academy of Creative Arts (Fall 2025)

Graduate Data and Research Consultant Fellowship

Description: During my fellowship at The Leadership Academy of Creative Arts as a Graduate Data and Research Consultant fellow, I partnered with organizational leaders to translate complex research into actionable insights for high-priority initiatives and modernize impact measurement strategies. I developed a clear path for organizational evaluation by creating a comprehensive logic model that maps out resources and activities for long-term outcomes. This foundational work has allowed TLACA to monitor real time impact, design a robust data framework on performance metrics, and drive continuous program improvement while guiding decision making.
Information Management (Spring 2026)

R-Reader Database Project

Description: The R-Reader Engine is a collaborative literacy platform that serves to capture the hidden value of literary discussions. This is created through a hybrid analytics approach to transform qualitative group dialogue into actionable data. There is no centralized system that tracks evolving engagement and emotional resonance in the reading community, leaving this data lost/unorganized, where these valuable insights aren’t tracked. This database project goal is to address the gap by utilizing a Vector Database and a hybrid NoSQL approach. By using R programming as the analytical engine, I will extract/pull data on public book reviews from Goodreads, Amazon, and Reddit to architect a “Search Engine” model. Bridging the gap between group analytics and raw data storage enables a semantic search, allowing users to discover books based on emotional themes and ”vibes” rather than direct keyword matches. Technical implementation involves a pipeline that starts with NoSQL schema linking meetings, members, and discussions. The use of R packages performs sentiment analysis to score the emotional tone of the text, which is fed into the Vectorization pipeline, converting texts into mathematical coordinates to map the thematic clusters. This system will allow it to function as a digital memory for the reading community while offering predictive insight to match exactly the type of book you are in the mood for. Served as a thematic needs of readers and an intelligence-driven platform that is proactive and understandable.
